Bitcoin: Warp Regression on a Single Macro Cycle¶

On the surface, Bitcoin looks like an easy forecasting problem. Everyone who follows it can point to the ~4-year halving cycle: boom, crash, quiet accumulation, repeat. Ask exactly when the next peak lands, though, and it gets vague fast. The cycles don't repeat on a fixed schedule: 2011 to 2013 to 2017 to 2021 to 2024 are all "about four years apart", but the actual spacing wanders by many months each time. Standard seasonality tools assume a fixed period, so they have nowhere to put that wander, and I'm not aware of a canonical, off-the-shelf method that answers "here's a roughly periodic series, tell me when the next cycle peaks and how confident you are" despite how simple the question sounds out loud.

That's exactly the gap warp regression is built for. Last stop on the tour: the synthetic notebook introduced the method, Lynx showed it working on real, noisy, annual data with two competing cycles. Now: daily Bitcoin closing prices, a series with basically one dominant story (the ~4-year halving cycle) riding on top of a trend that's grown by several orders of magnitude.

A few things make Bitcoin a genuinely different test case from Lynx:

  • Daily, not annual data. Thousands of observations instead of a hundred, so the warp path has a lot more freedom (14 knots vs 8, more room for the offset to wander day to day).
  • A strong trend dominates the level. We fit a log-trend first and warp the residual cycle around it, rather than warping the raw series.
  • The cycle count is known. Bitcoin's halving-driven boom/bust pattern has produced five recognisable macro peaks (2011, 2013, 2017, 2021, 2024). We pin the sine's phase and frequency directly to those, rather than grid-searching blind.
  • A closing act: after the warp model is fit, we'll add an optional residual-smoothing layer on top and check, honestly, whether it actually helps.

The model¶

$$y_t = C + B\log(t_{\text{idx}} - z) + f(t)\cdot\big(1 + d(1-t)\big)\cdot\mathrm{warp}\big(\sin(2\pi\omega t + \phi)\big)$$

where $y_t = \log(\text{price}_t)$. In words: a log-trend term, plus a warped macro sine whose amplitude is allowed to taper over time via a learnable envelope $1 + d(1-t)$ (the boom-bust swings were relatively larger early in Bitcoin's history).

Recap: the dual objective¶

Same training rule as the last two notebooks: fit_lambda ($\lambda$) trades off fit quality against warp plausibility:

loss = λ·obj_err − (1−λ)·obj_time        (λ = 0.9 here: daily data, long history, we let it chase fit)
  • Holdout split: last TEST_DAYS = 365 observations held out.
  • End-anchored warp: offset pinned to 0 at the train end; free to wander backward through history.
  • Endpoint presize: phase and time-scale are chosen so the first and last macro peaks land exactly on sine crests (five lobes across the training window). This is what lets us skip a blind frequency search.
  • Forecast: stochastic warp paths continued from the train-end anchor give terror and combined 95% bands, same joint-Monte-Carlo construction as before.
In [1]:
%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import torch

from warp_regression import (
    analyze_log_trend,
    plot_forecast_bands,
    plot_realisation_spaghetti,
    plot_warp_offset,
    prefit,
    stored_path_offset_numpy,
)
from warp_regression.readouts import log_trend_sine as btc

Step 1: load the full daily series¶

We use the complete blockchain.info daily USD close (cached locally, no date trimming). The warp learns on the full history; a 365-day holdout at the end is where we'll actually judge it.

In [2]:
df = btc.fetch_bitcoin_daily()
dates = pd.DatetimeIndex(pd.to_datetime(df["date"]))
price = df["price_usd"].to_numpy(dtype=np.float64)
n = len(price)

print(f"Observations: {n}")
print(f"Date range: {dates[0].date()} to {dates[-1].date()}")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 4))
ax.semilogy(dates, price, color="0.3", lw=0.9)
ax.set_title("BTC daily USD close (full series)")
ax.set_xlabel("date")
ax.set_ylabel("price USD")
btc.format_date_axis(ax, dates)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations: 5771
Date range: 2010-08-18 to 2026-06-05

Step 2: work in log space¶

$$y = \log(\text{price})$$

Bitcoin's price has grown across several orders of magnitude, and its swings are multiplicative (a "50% drawdown" means the same thing whether price is $100 or $60,000). Log-price puts that on an additive scale, which is what the warp model (and honestly, most time series models) expects.

In [3]:
y = btc.log_price(price)
t = btc.normalized_time(n)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 7), sharex=True)
axes[0].semilogy(dates, price, color="0.75", lw=0.8, label="price")
axes[0].set_ylabel("price USD")
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].set_title("Original price vs. log target")

axes[1].plot(dates, y, "k", lw=0.9, label="log price")
axes[1].set_ylabel("log(price)")
axes[1].set_xlabel("date")
axes[1].legend()
btc.format_date_axis(axes[1], dates)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Step 3: pull out the trend, then prefit the cycle on what's left¶

analyze_log_trend() fits and removes a log-trend from the training slice. Then prefit(..., n_sines=1) fits a single macro sine on the residual, using the five known macro peak years for phase alignment, rather than searching blind.

In [4]:
t_idx = btc.day_index(n)
split = btc.split_bitcoin_holdout(n, test_days=btc.TEST_DAYS, dates=dates)
train_idx, test_idx = split["train_idx"], split["test_idx"]
n_train = split["n_train"]
y_tr, y_te = y[train_idx], y[test_idx]
t_tr, t_te = t[train_idx], t[test_idx]
t_idx_tr = t_idx[train_idx]
dates_tr, dates_te = split["dates_train"], split["dates_test"]

print(f"Train: {dates_tr[0].date()} to {dates_tr[-1].date()} (n={n_train})")
print(f"Test:  {dates_te[0].date()} to {dates_te[-1].date()} (n={split['n_test']})")

prep = analyze_log_trend(y_tr, t_idx_tr)
peak_idx = btc.detect_btc_cycle_peaks(y_tr, dates_tr)
pf = prefit(
    prep.residual,
    t_tr,
    n_sines=1,
    peak_idx=peak_idx,
    omega=float(btc.N_CYCLE_PEAKS),
    envelope_init=True,
    t_full=t,
)
trend_pre = prep.trend
sine_fit = pf.sine_fit
z_pre = sine_fit["z"]
z_full = sine_fit["z_full"]
sine_fit_full = {**sine_fit, "z": z_full[:n_train]}

print(f"omega={sine_fit['omega']:.1f}  phase={sine_fit['phase']:.3f}  time_scale={sine_fit['time_scale']:.4f}")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 6), sharex=True)
ax0, ax1 = axes
ax0.plot(dates_tr, y_tr, color="0.45", lw=0.8, label="y_train (log)")
ax0.plot(dates_tr, trend_pre, color="black", lw=1.5, label="log trend (analyze)")
ax0.axvline(dates_tr[-1], color="gray", ls="--", lw=0.8)
ax0.set_ylabel("log price")
ax0.set_title("Log trend analysis on train")
ax0.legend(loc="upper left")
ax1.plot(dates, z_full, color="C1", lw=1.0, label="sine cycle z(t)")
ax1.axvline(dates_tr[-1], color="gray", ls="--", lw=0.8)
ax1.set_ylabel("z")
ax1.set_title("Sine prefit: phase from train residual, cycle shown on full calendar")
ax1.legend(loc="upper left")
btc.format_date_axis(ax1, dates)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Train: 2010-08-18 to 2025-06-05 (n=5406)
Test:  2025-06-06 to 2026-06-05 (n=365)

omega=5.0  phase=0.103  time_scale=0.9061

Step 4: fit the warp path and MLP output function¶

Jointly learn the B-spline knot values for p(i) and a small MLP output function on the warped sine, on train data only. fit_lambda=0.9 weights fit quality heavily. With this much daily history and a fairly constrained sine cycle, there's less risk of the warp overfitting than there was with Lynx's shorter annual series.

In [5]:
from warp_regression import per_index_rw_sigma

fit = btc.fit_warp_model(
    y_tr, t_idx_tr, t_tr, sine_fit_full,
    fit_lambda=btc.FIT_LAMBDA,
    epochs=btc.EPOCHS,
    n_knots=btc.N_KNOTS,
)
fit["_y_test"] = y_te

y_hat = fit["y_hat"]
n_knots = fit["n_knots"]
print(f"lambda          = {btc.FIT_LAMBDA}")
print(f"epochs          = {btc.EPOCHS}")
print(f"R2 (train)      = {fit['r2']:.4f}")
print(f"RMSE (train)    = {fit['rmse']:.4f}")
print(f"sigma_y         = {fit['warp']['sigma_y']:.4f}")
print(f"sigma_t         = {fit['warp']['sigma_t']:.4f}")
print(f"sigma_index     = {per_index_rw_sigma(fit['warp']['sigma_t'], n_train, n_knots):.4f}")
print(f"max offset      = {fit['warp']['max_abs_offset']:.1f} days")
print(f"offset@end      = {stored_path_offset_numpy(fit['warp']['p'])[-1]:.4f} (anchor)")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 4))
ax.plot(dates_tr, y_tr, "g.", ms=3, alpha=0.7, label="log price (train)")
ax.plot(dates_te, y_te, "ko", ms=3, alpha=0.45, label="log price (test)")
ax.plot(dates_tr, y_hat, color="C0", lw=1.5, label=f"warp fit (R²={fit['r2']:.3f})")
ax.axvline(split["train_end_date"], color="gray", ls="--", lw=0.8)
ax.set_title("Train-only warp + MLP fit")
ax.set_xlabel("date")
ax.set_ylabel("log(price)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9)
btc.format_date_axis(ax, dates)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

p_tr = fit["warp"]["p"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 3.5))
plot_warp_offset(
    ax, dates_tr, p_tr, anchor_x=split["train_end_date"], color="C2",
    label="warp offset (p−i)",
    title=f"End-anchored warp path (train)  max |offset| = {fit['warp']['max_abs_offset']:.1f}",
)
ax.set_ylabel("offset (days)")
ax.set_xlabel("date")
btc.format_date_axis(ax, dates)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

lambda          = 0.9
epochs          = 24000
R2 (train)      = 0.9871
RMSE (train)    = 0.3874
sigma_y         = 0.4039
sigma_t         = 0.2398
sigma_index     = 6.6630
max offset      = 375.0 days
offset@end      = 0.0000 (anchor)

Step 5: forecast the holdout¶

Same recipe as Lynx and the synthetic case: sample warp paths forward from the train-end anchor using the fitted $\sigma_t$, push each through the output function, and take percentiles for the terror band. The combined band adds independent $N(0, \sigma_y^2)$ noise to each path before taking percentiles: the joint Monte Carlo construction, not a naive interval sum.

In [6]:
from warp_regression import predict_forecast_realisations_torch

n_show = btc.N_SINGLE_PATHS
hist_start = max(0, n_train - int(2 * 365.25))
dates_hist = dates[hist_start:]

fc = predict_forecast_realisations_torch(
    fit=fit,
    t_idx=t_idx,
    t_norm=t,
    z_full=z_full,
    n_obs=n_train,
    n_future=split["n_test"],
    n_draws=n_show,
    seed=43,
    noise_seed=44,
)
bands = fc["bands"]
y_point = fc["y_point"]
preds = fc["preds"][:n_show]

print(f"Point forecast train/test corr = {np.corrcoef(y_point[:n_train], fit['y_hat'])[0, 1]:.3f} / {np.corrcoef(y_point[test_idx], y_te)[0, 1]:.3f}")
print(f"Test RMSE (log) = {np.sqrt(np.mean((y_point[test_idx] - y_te) ** 2)):.4f}")
in_combined = (y_te >= bands["c_q_lo"][test_idx]) & (y_te <= bands["c_q_hi"][test_idx])
print(f"Combined 95% coverage on test = {in_combined.mean():.1%}")

bands_test = {k: bands[k][test_idx] for k in ("t_q_lo", "t_q_hi", "c_q_lo", "c_q_hi")}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 8), sharex=True)

plot_realisation_spaghetti(
    axes[0], dates_hist, preds[:, hist_start:], dates_hist, y_point[hist_start:],
    x_obs=dates[hist_start:n_train], y_obs=y[hist_start:n_train], obs_label="train",
    x_test=dates_te, y_test=y_te, test_label="test (holdout)",
    forecast_start_x=split["train_end_date"], path_alpha=0.5, path_lw=0.9,
    ylabel="log(price)",
    title=f"{preds.shape[0]} forecast realisations (sampled warp paths via fitted output function)",
)

plot_forecast_bands(
    axes[1], dates[hist_start:n_train], y[hist_start:n_train], dates_te, bands_test,
    x_test=dates_te, y_test=y_te,
    point_lines=[(dates_hist, y_point[hist_start:], {"color": "black", "lw": 1.5, "label": "point forecast"})],
    forecast_start_x=split["train_end_date"],
    train_label="train", test_label="test (holdout)",
    xlabel="date", ylabel="log(price)",
    title=(
        f"Uncertainty bands  σ_t={fc['sigma_t']:.3f}  σ_y={fc['sigma_y']:.3f}  "
        f"(σ_index={per_index_rw_sigma(fc['sigma_t'], n_train, n_knots):.3f})"
    ),
)
btc.format_date_axis(axes[1], dates_hist)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Point forecast train/test corr = 1.000 / 0.888
Test RMSE (log) = 0.1693
Combined 95% coverage on test = 100.0%

Step 6: the 5-year forecast¶

The 365-day holdout is a fair test, but the real curiosity with a macro-cycle model is the multi-year view. Refit on the entire series (no holdout this time) and push the forecast out five years. Take the uncertainty bands with an appropriately large grain of salt this far out, but this is exactly the kind of long-horizon, cycle-aware forecast that motivated warp regression in the first place.

In [7]:
print("Full-series presize + warp fit for 5-year forecast")
prep_all = analyze_log_trend(y, t_idx)
peak_all = btc.detect_btc_cycle_peaks(y, dates)
pf_all = prefit(
    prep_all.residual, t, n_sines=1, peak_idx=peak_all,
    omega=float(btc.N_CYCLE_PEAKS), envelope_init=True, t_full=t,
)
sine_fit_all = pf_all.sine_fit
z_all = sine_fit_all["z_full"]
sine_fit_all_obs = {**sine_fit_all, "z": z_all}

fit_full = btc.fit_warp_model(
    y, t_idx, t, sine_fit_all_obs,
    fit_lambda=btc.FIT_LAMBDA,
    epochs=btc.EPOCHS,
    n_knots=btc.N_KNOTS,
)
n_knots_full = fit_full["n_knots"]
print(f"Full-series R2 = {fit_full['r2']:.4f}  sigma_y = {fit_full['warp']['sigma_y']:.4f}  sigma_t = {fit_full['warp']['sigma_t']:.4f}")

n_future_5 = btc.FORECAST_5YR_DAYS
t_ext, t_idx_ext, z_ext, n_total_5 = btc.build_forecast_extension(n, n_future_5, sine_fit_all)
dates_ext = pd.date_range(dates[0], periods=n_total_5, freq="D")
dates_fut_5 = dates_ext[n:]

fc5 = predict_forecast_realisations_torch(
    fit=fit_full,
    t_idx=t_idx_ext,
    t_norm=t_ext,
    z_full=z_ext,
    n_obs=n,
    n_future=n_future_5,
    n_draws=n_show,
    seed=101,
    noise_seed=102,
)
bands5 = fc5["bands"]
y_point5 = fc5["y_point"]
preds5 = fc5["preds"][:n_show]

hist_start_5 = max(0, n - int(3 * 365.25))
fut_sl = np.arange(n, n_total_5)
print(f"Forecast horizon: {dates[-1].date()} to {dates_fut_5[-1].date()}  ({n_future_5} days)")
print(f"Point forecast at +5yr (log) = {y_point5[-1]:.3f}  price ~ ${np.exp(y_point5[-1]):,.0f}")

bands5_fut = {k: bands5[k][fut_sl] for k in ("t_q_lo", "t_q_hi", "c_q_lo", "c_q_hi")}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 9), sharex=True)

plot_realisation_spaghetti(
    axes[0], dates_ext[hist_start_5:], preds5[:, hist_start_5:], dates_ext[hist_start_5:], y_point5[hist_start_5:],
    x_obs=dates[hist_start_5:n], y_obs=y[hist_start_5:n], obs_label="observed",
    forecast_start_x=dates[-1], path_alpha=0.5, path_lw=0.9,
    ylabel="log(price)",
    title=f"5-year forward forecast (full-series fit): {preds5.shape[0]} knot-smooth realisations",
)

plot_forecast_bands(
    axes[1], dates[hist_start_5:n], y[hist_start_5:n], dates_fut_5, bands5_fut,
    point_lines=[(dates_ext[hist_start_5:], y_point5[hist_start_5:], {"color": "black", "lw": 1.5, "label": "point forecast"})],
    forecast_start_x=dates[-1],
    train_label="observed",
    xlabel="date", ylabel="log(price)",
    title=(
        f"5-year uncertainty bands  σ_t={fc5['sigma_t']:.3f}  σ_y={fc5['sigma_y']:.3f}  "
        f"(σ_index={per_index_rw_sigma(fc5['sigma_t'], n, n_knots_full):.3f})"
    ),
)
btc.format_date_axis(axes[1], dates_ext[hist_start_5:])
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))
ax.semilogy(dates[hist_start_5:n], price[hist_start_5:n], color="0.75", lw=0.9, label="observed")
for k in range(preds5.shape[0]):
    ax.semilogy(dates_ext[hist_start_5:], np.exp(preds5[k, hist_start_5:]), alpha=0.5, lw=0.9, color="C0")
ax.semilogy(dates_ext[hist_start_5:], np.exp(y_point5[hist_start_5:]), "k", lw=2, label="point forecast")
ax.axvline(dates[-1], color="gray", ls="--", lw=0.9, label="last observation")
ax.set_ylabel("price USD")
ax.set_xlabel("date")
ax.set_title("5-year forward forecast (price scale, full-series fit)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left")
btc.format_date_axis(ax, dates_ext[hist_start_5:])
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Full-series presize + warp fit for 5-year forecast

Full-series R2 = 0.9832  sigma_y = 0.3686  sigma_t = 0.2356

Forecast horizon: 2026-06-05 to 2031-06-05  (1826 days)
Point forecast at +5yr (log) = 12.320  price ~ $224,157

Step 7: how long until the next halving-cycle peak?¶

Same trick as the last two notebooks: continue warp paths forward with the fitted $\sigma_t$ and measure the spacing to the next peak of the warped macro sine. The dashed line is the nominal cycle length $n/\omega$ on the training calendar: what you'd get if the cycle were perfectly regular.

In [8]:
from warp_regression import analyze_cycle_lengths, plot_cycle_length_distribution

cyc = analyze_cycle_lengths(
    fit=fit_full,
    sine_fit=sine_fit_all,
    n_calendar=n,
    unit="days",
    n_paths=500,
    seed=7,
)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
plot_cycle_length_distribution(cyc, ax=ax, title="Next Bitcoin cycle length (warp terror)")
print(
    f"Nominal cycle ≈ {cyc.mean_cycle_length:.0f} days  |  "
    f"sample median = {np.median(cyc.lengths):.0f} days  |  "
    f"σ_t = {cyc.sigma_t:.3f}"
)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Nominal cycle ≈ 1308 days  |  sample median = 988 days  |  σ_t = 0.236

Step 8: does the residual layer help?¶

One honest question worth asking of any forecast: are the leftover errors random, or is there a persistent bias the model is missing? After fitting the warp model, the training residuals $y_{\text{train}} - \hat y_{\text{train}}$ might carry a systematic level, a hint that the point forecast is running a bit hot or cold relative to reality.

The simplest possible fix: take a causal rolling mean over the last $h$ residuals (where $h$ is the forecast horizon), and extrapolate that flat level into the future as an additive correction. No exponential smoothing, no trend term, just "what's the recent average miss, and does it keep missing that way":

  • In-sample: the rolling mean tracks the residual level through training.
  • Out-of-sample: a flat correction at level = mean(residuals[-h:]), applied to every future step.
  • Bands: combined CI width grows to account for σ_MA = std(residuals[-h:]) as well as σ_y.

Let's see whether it earns its keep on this holdout.

In [9]:
from warp_regression import apply_residual_forecast, fit_residual_smooth
from warp_regression.residual_smooth import forecast_residual_std

# Fit the MA layer on warp training residuals
residuals_tr = y_tr - y_hat
res_fit = fit_residual_smooth(residuals_tr, horizon=split["n_test"])
print(f"MA residual layer  (window = {res_fit.horizon} steps)")
print(f"  level (mean of last {res_fit.horizon} residuals) = {res_fit.level:+.4f}")
print(f"  sigma (std  of last {res_fit.horizon} residuals) = {res_fit.sigma:.4f}")

# Apply it as a correction on top of the existing holdout forecast
sigma_y = fc["sigma_y"]
fc_ma = apply_residual_forecast(fc, res_fit, n_train, sigma_y)
y_point_ma = fc_ma["y_point"]
bands_ma = fc_ma["bands"]

rmse_base = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y_point[test_idx] - y_te) ** 2)))
rmse_ma = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((y_point_ma[test_idx] - y_te) ** 2)))
cov_base = float(np.mean((y_te >= bands["c_q_lo"][test_idx]) & (y_te <= bands["c_q_hi"][test_idx])))
cov_ma = float(np.mean((y_te >= bands_ma["c_q_lo"][test_idx]) & (y_te <= bands_ma["c_q_hi"][test_idx])))
print(f"\nHoldout RMSE:           base={rmse_base:.4f}  |  MA-corrected={rmse_ma:.4f}")
print(f"Combined 95% coverage:  base={cov_base:.1%}  |  MA-corrected={cov_ma:.1%}")

MA residual layer  (window = 365 steps)
  level (mean of last 365 residuals) = -0.0531
  sigma (std  of last 365 residuals) = 0.1245

Holdout RMSE:           base=0.1693  |  MA-corrected=0.1456
Combined 95% coverage:  base=100.0%  |  MA-corrected=100.0%
In [10]:
# Comparison plot: in-sample fit, then holdout forecast, base vs MA-corrected
hist_start_ma = max(0, n_train - int(2 * 365.25))
dates_h = dates[hist_start_ma:]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 9), sharex=True)

ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(dates_tr[hist_start_ma:], y_tr[hist_start_ma:], color="0.6", lw=0.9, label="observed (train)")
ax.plot(dates_te, y_te, "ko", ms=3, alpha=0.5, label="observed (test)")
ax.plot(dates_tr[hist_start_ma:], y_hat[hist_start_ma:], color="steelblue", lw=1.5, label="warp fit")
ax.plot(
    dates_tr[hist_start_ma:],
    (y_hat + res_fit.fitted)[hist_start_ma:],
    color="C1", lw=1.5, ls="--", label=f"warp + MA correction (window={res_fit.horizon})"
)
ax.axvline(split["train_end_date"], color="gray", ls="--", lw=0.9, label="forecast start")
ax.set_ylabel("log(price)")
ax.set_title("In-sample fit: warp vs warp + MA residual correction")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left")

ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(dates[hist_start_ma:n_train], y[hist_start_ma:n_train], color="0.6", lw=0.9, label="observed (train)")
ax.plot(dates_te, y_te, "ko", ms=3, alpha=0.5, label="observed (test)")
ax.plot(dates_h[n_train - hist_start_ma:], y_point[n_train:], color="steelblue", lw=1.8, label="point forecast (base)")
ax.fill_between(
    dates_te, bands["c_q_lo"][test_idx], bands["c_q_hi"][test_idx],
    color="steelblue", alpha=0.15, label="combined 95% CI (base)"
)
ax.plot(dates_h[n_train - hist_start_ma:], y_point_ma[n_train:], color="C1", lw=1.8, ls="--", label="point forecast (MA)")
ax.fill_between(
    dates_te, bands_ma["c_q_lo"][test_idx], bands_ma["c_q_hi"][test_idx],
    color="C1", alpha=0.15, label="combined 95% CI (MA)"
)
ax.axvline(split["train_end_date"], color="gray", ls="--", lw=0.9)
ax.set_ylabel("log(price)")
ax.set_xlabel("date")
ax.set_title(f"Holdout forecast: base vs MA-corrected  (RMSE base={rmse_base:.4f} / MA={rmse_ma:.4f})")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left")
btc.format_date_axis(ax, dates_h)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [11]:
# Residual detail: what the MA layer actually saw, and what it extrapolated
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 5), sharex=True)
ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(dates_tr, residuals_tr, color="0.5", lw=0.7, alpha=0.7, label="train residuals")
ax.plot(dates_tr, res_fit.fitted, color="C1", lw=1.5, label=f"rolling MA (window={res_fit.horizon})")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.5)
ax.set_ylabel("residual")
ax.set_title("Rolling-MA smoother on warp training residuals")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax = axes[1]
h_fwd = np.arange(1, len(test_idx) + 1)
res_std = forecast_residual_std(res_fit, h_fwd)
ax.axhline(res_fit.level, color="C1", lw=1.5, label=f"flat forecast = {res_fit.level:+.4f}")
ax.fill_between(dates_te, res_fit.level - 1.96 * res_std, res_fit.level + 1.96 * res_std,
                color="C1", alpha=0.2, label="±1.96·σ_MA")
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.5)
ax.set_ylabel("residual forecast")
ax.set_xlabel("date")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
btc.format_date_axis(ax, dates_te)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Wrapping up¶

Step What happened
1–2 Loaded daily BTC prices, moved to log space
3 Removed the log-trend, prefit a macro sine on the residual using known peak years
4 Fit the warp path + MLP output function on train only (λ=0.9)
5 Forecast the 365-day holdout with terror + combined 95% bands
6 Refit on the full series, pushed the forecast out 5 years
7 Distribution over the next cycle's length
8 Tested a simple moving-average residual correction, honestly, for and against

That's the whole arc across three notebooks: a clean synthetic example to see the mechanics, a real ecological series to stress-test it with multiple cycles and nonlinear output functions, and a financial series with a dominant trend and a single macro cycle. Same core idea throughout: a known-shape cycle, a warp path with its own likelihood, and forecast uncertainty that comes directly from sampling that likelihood forward in time.
